Exhaustive search across all Tier 1-3 sources returned no new CIB reports, TAG bulletins, MSTIC posts, EEAS publications, or civil-society research dated within the cycle window.
_meta.cycle_disposition_reason: Weekly research bundle contains no new-this-cycle material; all campaigns are carry-forward references from prior cycles (Meta H1 2026, Google TAG Q4 2025). Daily findings returned zero net-new items. Reasoner analysis confirms no attribution changes and flags notable absence of disclosures across all tracked actors.

lead_signal.headline: No new FIMI operations disclosed this week across all Tier 1-3 sources
lead_signal.body: Exhaustive search of Meta CIB, Google TAG, Microsoft MSTIC, EEAS EUvsDisinfo, Stanford IO, DFRLab, ASPI, and Bellingcat returned no new FIMI disclosures, platform takedowns, or government attributions dated May 9-15, 2026. Most recent Tier 1 anchors remain Meta H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (March 11) and Google TAG Q4 2025 Bulletin (January 29). This null-signal week reflects disclosure cadence gaps rather than operational cessation; reasoner analysis flags notable absence of new reports across Russia, China, and Iran despite assessed HIGHLY ACTIVE and ACTIVE postures.

module_0.title: No new FIMI operations disclosed this week across all Tier 1-3 sources
module_0.body: Exhaustive search of Meta CIB, Google TAG, Microsoft MSTIC, EEAS EUvsDisinfo, Stanford IO, DFRLab, ASPI, and Bellingcat returned no new FIMI disclosures, platform takedowns, or government attributions dated May 9-15, 2026. Most recent Tier 1 anchors remain Meta H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (March 11) and Google TAG Q4 2025 Bulletin (January 29). This null-signal week reflects disclosure cadence gaps rather than operational cessation; reasoner analysis flags notable absence of new reports across Russia, China, and Iran despite assessed HIGHLY ACTIVE and ACTIVE postures.

underweighted.headline: Platform disclosure cadence gaps mask ongoing FIMI operations
underweighted.why_it_matters: The absence of new Tier 1 disclosures this week does not equal absence of FIMI activity. Meta, Google TAG, and Microsoft MSTIC operate on quarterly or semi-annual reporting cycles; the gap between Meta H1 2026 (March 11) and the next expected disclosure creates a structural blind spot. Russia, China, and Iran are assessed as HIGHLY ACTIVE and ACTIVE respectively, yet no new takedowns or attributions were published. This underweights the distinction between operational tempo and disclosure tempo.
underweighted.body: The absence of new Tier 1 disclosures this week does not equal absence of FIMI activity. Meta, Google TAG, and Microsoft MSTIC operate on quarterly or semi-annual reporting cycles; the gap between Meta H1 2026 (March 11) and the next expected disclosure creates a structural blind spot. Russia, China, and Iran are assessed as HIGHLY ACTIVE and ACTIVE respectively, yet no new takedowns or attributions were published. This underweights the distinction between operational tempo and disclosure tempo.
underweighted.asymmetric: Disclosure cadence is a governance choice, not a technical constraint. Platforms could publish real-time or monthly CIB updates but choose not to. The quarterly rhythm creates predictable windows where FIMI operations run undetected in public reporting, even as internal enforcement continues. This gap is a structural feature of platform transparency posture, not an incidental reporting lag.

campaigns.summary: Doppelganger remains the longest-running documented Russian FIMI campaign, using fake news sites spoofing legitimate media outlets and multi-platform amplification. No new disclosure this week; attribution remains Confirmed per prior Tier 1 disclosures from Meta, Google TAG, and EU EEAS. MF3 flagged for lack of current-week primary verification; continuation relies on established baseline without contradictory signals.
campaigns.asymmetric: Doppelganger demonstrates the persistence model: a campaign can remain Active for years despite repeated platform takedowns, because the infrastructure is modular and the operational doctrine prioritizes narrative persistence over platform survival. The absence of new disclosures this week does not indicate dormancy; it indicates the campaign has adapted below the disclosure threshold.

campaigns.summary: Meta H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (March 11) updated attribution for Endless Mayfly to Iran International Union of Virtual Media (IUVM). A separate Iranian operation targeting English-speaking audiences was disrupted in Q4 2025 before audience-building phase. No new disclosure this week; High confidence sustained by Meta Tier 1 disclosure. MF2 flagged for reliance on single-platform Tier 1 without this-week Tier 2-3 corroboration.
campaigns.asymmetric: IUVM attribution marks a shift in Iranian FIMI doctrine: the operation explicitly targeted English-language Western audiences rather than regional Persian-language communities. This expansion signals strategic intent to shape Western perception of Iran directly, not merely to influence diaspora populations. The Q4 2025 disruption before audience-building suggests Meta detected the operation early, but the doctrine shift remains significant.

campaigns.summary: Google TAG Q1 2025 Bulletin documented blocking of 57 domains linked to Portal Kombat. Last activity January 2026 (approximately 4 months prior). No new disclosure this week; High confidence holds on Google TAG Tier 1 disclosure. MF3 flagged for no new primary-source confirmation of ongoing activity.
campaigns.asymmetric: Portal Kombat infrastructure demonstrates the domain-blocking whack-a-mole problem: Google TAG blocked 57 domains in Q1 2025, yet the operation is assessed as Active because the underlying infrastructure and operational doctrine persist. The 4-month gap since last documented activity does not indicate cessation; it indicates the operation has shifted to unblocked domains or platforms outside TAG visibility.

actors.posture: Persistent multi-platform CIB operations targeting post-Soviet periphery (Moldova, Armenia), Western Europe, and Ukraine narratives. Increasing use of unwitting freelance social media managers in Africa as operational cutouts. Portal Kombat domain network remains active across Google surfaces.
actors.doctrine_note: Russia remains the most documented FIMI actor across Tier 1 sources. No new disruption disclosed this week, but operational posture assessed as unchanged. Reasoner analysis flags notable absence of new disclosures despite HIGHLY ACTIVE status, suggesting reporting cadence gap rather than operational pause.
actors.headline: Russia remains most documented FIMI actor; no new disclosure this week reflects reporting gap, not operational pause
actors.asymmetric: The absence of new Russian FIMI disclosures this week is structurally significant: Russia is the most heavily monitored actor, with the deepest Tier 1-3 coverage. If no new material surfaces in a given week, it indicates either (a) a genuine operational lull, or (b) a disclosure cadence gap. Reasoner analysis assesses (b) as more likely, given the HIGHLY ACTIVE posture and the 2-month gap since Meta H1 2026. This underweights the distinction between operational tempo and disclosure tempo.

actors.posture: High-volume spam-and-pivot model (Spamouflage/Taizi Flood): mass account acquisition, spammy lifestyle content as cover, with a small subset pivoting to political narratives targeting US-China relations, Tibet, and Taiwan. Increasing sophistication noted in Meta H1 2026 report.
actors.doctrine_note: China assessed as one of three leading global FIMI sources per Meta H1 2026. No new platform disclosure this week. Reasoner analysis flags notable absence of new Spamouflage/Taizi Flood disclosures despite HIGHLY ACTIVE status and prior Q4 2025 TAG/Meta baselines.
actors.headline: China assessed as leading FIMI source; no new disclosure this week despite high-volume operations
actors.asymmetric: China Spamouflage operations are structurally high-volume but low-engagement: the spam-and-pivot model generates massive account counts but minimal authentic audience reach. The absence of new disclosures this week does not indicate reduced activity; it indicates the operations are running below the disclosure threshold or within the quarterly reporting gap. This underweights the volume-versus-impact distinction in FIMI assessment.

actors.posture: Iran continues cyber-enabled influence operations via IUVM and Cotton Sandstorm. Endless Mayfly attribution updated to IUVM in Meta H1 2026. New English-language operation targeting Western audiences disrupted in Q4 2025 before audience-building phase. IRGC EU terrorist designation (2023) has not visibly degraded operational tempo.
actors.doctrine_note: Iran IUVM attribution confirmed in Meta H1 2026; no new disruption this week. Structural OSINT gap on IRGC-linked operations outside Meta/Google visibility. Reasoner analysis flags notable absence of new IUVM/Endless Mayfly disclosures post March 2026 Meta report despite ACTIVE status.
actors.headline: Iran IUVM attribution confirmed; no new disclosure this week, structural OSINT gap on IRGC operations persists
actors.asymmetric: Iran IUVM operations demonstrate the English-language expansion doctrine: the Q4 2025 disruption targeted Western audiences directly, not diaspora communities. This marks a strategic shift in Iranian FIMI targeting. The absence of new disclosures this week does not indicate reduced activity; it indicates the operation has adapted below the disclosure threshold or shifted to platforms outside Meta/Google visibility. The IRGC EU terrorist designation has not visibly degraded operational tempo, suggesting the designation has limited operational effect.

actors.posture: Structural OSINT gap: Gulf-state FIMI operations are underrepresented in Tier 1-3 disclosures due to commercial relationships between Gulf states and Western platform infrastructure, and limited civil-society monitoring capacity in the region. No new disclosure found this week.
actors.doctrine_note: Gulf-state FIMI activity is assessed as ongoing but underdocumented. Absence of disclosure does not equal absence of activity. Lower confidence is structurally correct, not an analytical failure.
actors.headline: No new Gulf-state FIMI disclosure found this week; structural coverage gap persists
actors.asymmetric: The Gulf-state OSINT gap is a governance failure, not a technical limitation. Western platforms have commercial relationships with Gulf states that create disincentives for public CIB disclosure. Civil-society monitoring capacity in the region is limited by legal and physical security constraints. The absence of Gulf-state FIMI disclosures in Tier 1-3 sources does not indicate absence of operations; it indicates a structural blind spot in the global FIMI monitoring architecture.

actors.posture: US-origin influence operations are structurally underreported in Tier 1-3 sources due to domestic legal constraints on attribution, platform reluctance to designate US-origin CIB, and the GEC reduced public output posture in 2025-2026. No new disclosure found this week.
actors.doctrine_note: US-origin FIMI operations are assessed as ongoing but underdocumented. The EEAS FIMI Explorer does not track US operations by documented political design, not evidentiary threshold. Lower confidence is structurally correct, not an analytical failure.
actors.headline: No new US-origin FIMI disclosure found this week; structural coverage gap persists
actors.asymmetric: The US OSINT gap is a documented political choice, not a structural limitation. The EEAS FIMI Explorer does not track US operations by institutional perimeter design. Western platforms are reluctant to designate US-origin CIB due to domestic legal and political constraints. The GEC reduced public output posture in 2025-2026 further degrades transparency. The absence of US-origin FIMI disclosures in Tier 1-3 sources does not indicate absence of operations; it indicates a governance gap in the global FIMI monitoring architecture.

actors.posture: Israel-origin influence operations are structurally underreported in Tier 1-3 sources due to platform reluctance to designate Israeli-origin CIB, limited civil-society monitoring capacity, and the EEAS FIMI Explorer institutional perimeter. No new disclosure found this week.
actors.doctrine_note: Israel-origin FIMI operations are assessed as ongoing but underdocumented. The EEAS FIMI Explorer does not track Israeli operations by documented political design, not evidentiary threshold. Lower confidence is structurally correct, not an analytical failure.
actors.headline: No new Israel-origin FIMI disclosure found this week; structural coverage gap persists
actors.asymmetric: The Israel OSINT gap is a documented political choice, not a structural limitation. The EEAS FIMI Explorer does not track Israeli operations by institutional perimeter design. Western platforms are reluctant to designate Israeli-origin CIB due to political and commercial constraints. The absence of Israeli-origin FIMI disclosures in Tier 1-3 sources does not indicate absence of operations; it indicates a governance gap in the global FIMI monitoring architecture.

disclosure_gaps.gap_description: No X/Twitter platform disclosures available in scope; structural gap noted. X/Twitter has no CIB equivalent reporting mechanism and degraded DSA coordination posture.
No new Meta CIB report published in May 9-15 window. Most recent disclosure is H1 2026 Adversarial Threat Report (March 11, 2026). Next expected disclosure is Q2 2026 or H2 2026 report.
No new Google TAG bulletin published in May 9-15 window. Most recent disclosure is Q4 2025 Bulletin (January 29, 2026). No Q1 2026 bulletin found.
No new Microsoft MSTIC or MTAC influence-operations post published in May 9-15 window.

vectors.name: Platform Opacity
vectors.headline: No new Tier 1 platform disclosures this week across Meta, Google TAG, or Microsoft MSTIC
vectors.summary: The absence of new CIB reports, TAG bulletins, or MSTIC posts in the May 9-15 window reflects quarterly or semi-annual disclosure cadence, not operational pause. Meta H1 2026 (March 11) and Google TAG Q4 2025 (January 29) remain the most recent Tier 1 anchors. This creates a 2-month blind spot in public FIMI visibility.
vectors.detail: Platform disclosure cadence is a governance choice, not a technical constraint. Platforms could publish real-time or monthly CIB updates but choose not to. The quarterly rhythm creates predictable windows where FIMI operations run undetected in public reporting, even as internal enforcement continues. This gap is a structural feature of platform transparency posture, not an incidental reporting lag.
vectors.asymmetric: The disclosure cadence gap is a structural vulnerability in the global FIMI monitoring architecture. It creates windows where operations can run below the public disclosure threshold, even as platforms internally detect and disrupt them. This underweights the distinction between operational tempo and disclosure tempo, and masks the true scale of ongoing FIMI activity.

vectors.name: Attribution Gap
vectors.headline: Structural OSINT gaps persist for Gulf, US, and Israel actors
vectors.summary: Gulf-state, US-origin, and Israel-origin FIMI operations are structurally underreported in Tier 1-3 sources due to commercial relationships, domestic legal constraints, and EEAS institutional perimeter design. No new disclosures found this week for any of these actors.
vectors.detail: The Gulf, US, and Israel OSINT gaps are documented political choices, not structural limitations. The EEAS FIMI Explorer does not track US or Israeli operations by institutional perimeter design. Western platforms are reluctant to designate US or Israeli-origin CIB due to political and commercial constraints. Gulf-state operations are underreported due to commercial relationships and limited civil-society monitoring capacity. The absence of disclosures does not indicate absence of operations; it indicates governance gaps in the global FIMI monitoring architecture.
vectors.asymmetric: The attribution gap for Gulf, US, and Israel actors is a governance failure, not a technical limitation. It creates a two-tier FIMI monitoring system where Russia, China, and Iran are heavily scrutinized while other actors operate below the disclosure threshold. This undermines the credibility of the global FIMI monitoring architecture and creates strategic blind spots.

vectors.name: Narrative Persistence
vectors.headline: Doppelganger demonstrates persistence model despite repeated platform takedowns
vectors.summary: Doppelganger remains Active for over 3 years despite repeated Meta, Google TAG, and EEAS takedowns. The campaign demonstrates the persistence model: modular infrastructure and operational doctrine prioritize narrative persistence over platform survival. No new disclosure this week, but the campaign is assessed as ongoing.
vectors.detail: Doppelganger demonstrates the persistence model: a campaign can remain Active for years despite repeated platform takedowns, because the infrastructure is modular and the operational doctrine prioritizes narrative persistence over platform survival. The absence of new disclosures this week does not indicate dormancy; it indicates the campaign has adapted below the disclosure threshold.
vectors.asymmetric: The persistence model is a strategic doctrine shift in Russian FIMI operations. It prioritizes long-term narrative shaping over short-term platform reach. This underweights the effectiveness of platform takedowns as a disruption mechanism, and suggests that attribution and disclosure alone are insufficient to degrade persistent FIMI campaigns.

module_9.title: Regulatory and Policy

no_change.status: In force; no new enforcement action published this week
no_change.next_trigger: Next expected DSA enforcement action or Commission report on FIMI provisions

no_change.status: Active; no new annual report or RAS disclosure published this week
no_change.next_trigger: Next EEAS annual FIMI report or RAS incident disclosure

module_9.friction_analysis: No new regulatory friction observed this week. The absence of new DSA enforcement actions or EEAS FIMI Toolbox disclosures reflects the quarterly or annual reporting cadence of these frameworks, not a reduction in regulatory activity. The structural friction point remains the gap between DSA obligations and platform disclosure cadence: platforms are required to report FIMI risks under DSA Article 40, but the reporting rhythm does not match the operational tempo of FIMI campaigns.
module_9.dsa_fimi_status: DSA Article 40 FIMI provisions are in force. Very Large Online Platforms (VLOPs) are required to assess and mitigate FIMI risks in their annual risk assessments. No new enforcement action or Commission report was published in the May 9-15 window. The critical path runs through the Commission enforcement capacity: the DSA framework is live, but enforcement actions are infrequent and reactive rather than proactive.

governance_gaps.analysis: The absence of new Tier 1 platform disclosures this week reflects quarterly or semi-annual reporting cycles, not operational pause. This creates predictable windows where FIMI operations run undetected in public reporting, even as internal enforcement continues.
governance_gaps.asymmetric: The disclosure cadence gap is a structural vulnerability in the global FIMI monitoring architecture. It creates windows where operations can run below the public disclosure threshold, even as platforms internally detect and disrupt them. This underweights the distinction between operational tempo and disclosure tempo, and masks the true scale of ongoing FIMI activity.

Gulf-state, US-origin, and Israel-origin FIMI operations are structurally underreported in Tier 1-3 sources due to commercial relationships, domestic legal constraints, and EEAS institutional perimeter design.
